items.mistake: Treating D_KL(P || Q) and D_KL(Q || P) as interchangeable
items.fix: Always state which distribution is sampled (numerator expectation) and which is the reference in the denominator of the log ratio.
items.why: They optimize different objectives — forward KL is mode-seeking; reverse KL is mean-seeking — and can yield very different fitted Q.
items.example: For a bimodal P, minimizing D_KL(P||Q) may pick one mode; minimizing D_KL(Q||P) may spread Q across both modes.

items.mistake: Computing KL without checking support overlap
items.fix: Ensure q(x) > 0 wherever p(x) > 0, or use explicit handling (masking, smoothing, or reporting infinity).
items.why: A single zero in q where p is positive makes log(p/q) infinite; silent clipping can hide a broken model.
items.example: If p = [0.5, 0.5] and q = [1.0, 0.0], D_KL(P||Q) = ∞, not a small finite number from `log(0.5/1e-12)` alone without renormalization.

items.mistake: Confusing KL with symmetric distances like total variation or JS divergence
items.fix: Use Jensen–Shannon or Hellinger when a symmetric divergence is needed; keep KL when the directed information-theoretic meaning is intended.
items.why: Averaging or symmetrizing KL without understanding changes the optimization behavior and interpretation.
items.example: JS(P,Q) = 0.5 D_KL(P||M) + 0.5 D_KL(Q||M) with M = (P+Q)/2 is symmetric; plain KL is not.
